question: How does an LLM generate text token by token, and what's the difference between greedy decoding, sampling, and beam search?
answer: An LLM predicts a probability distribution over the next token given all previous tokens, then that token is chosen and appended to the sequence, repeating the process. Greedy decoding always picks the single most probable next token. Sampling (with temperature/top-p) draws randomly from the probability distribution for more diverse output. Beam search tracks several candidate sequences in parallel, keeping the most probable overall sequences rather than committing greedily at each step.
explanation: Greedy decoding is fast but can get stuck in repetitive or bland text since it never explores alternatives. Sampling introduces controlled randomness — temperature scales how 'peaked' vs 'flat' the distribution is before sampling, and top-p (nucleus sampling) restricts sampling to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Beam search is common in translation/summarization where a single best overall sequence matters more than diversity.
example: At temperature 0, generation becomes deterministic (equivalent to greedy); at high temperature, output becomes more random and creative but less coherent.
code: output = model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, temperature=0.7, top_p=0.9, max_new_tokens=100)
real_world: Chat assistants typically use sampling with moderate temperature and top-p for natural, varied conversational responses, while code generation often uses lower temperature for more deterministic, reliable output.
interview_question: Why might greedy decoding produce noticeably worse, more repetitive text than sampling-based decoding for open-ended creative writing?
references: Holtzman et al., The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration, 2019
memory_tip: Greedy = always the safest next word (can be boring/repetitive). Sampling = controlled randomness for variety. Beam search = track several full sentences, keep the best one overall.
common_mistake: Using temperature 0 (fully greedy/deterministic) for creative tasks and getting repetitive, degenerate output.

question: What is subword tokenization (e.g. Byte-Pair Encoding), and why do LLMs use it instead of word-level or character-level tokenization?
answer: Subword tokenization breaks text into frequently-occurring chunks smaller than whole words but larger than single characters, built by iteratively merging the most common adjacent symbol pairs in a training corpus. It balances vocabulary size against sequence length: word-level tokenization has an enormous vocabulary and can't handle unseen words, while character-level tokenization keeps vocabulary tiny but makes sequences very long and harder to learn from.
explanation: BPE (and similar methods like WordPiece, SentencePiece) let common words remain a single token while rare or novel words get split into meaningful subword pieces, so the model never encounters a truly 'unknown' token and can still generalize to made-up or rare words by composing familiar subword units.
example: The word 'unbelievable' might tokenize as ['un', 'believ', 'able'], letting the model reuse the 'un' and 'able' pieces it has seen in thousands of other words.
code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('gpt2')
tok.tokenize('unbelievable')  # ['un', 'bel', 'iev', 'able']
real_world: Every major LLM (GPT, Claude, Llama) uses some form of subword tokenization, which is also why LLMs sometimes struggle with character-level tasks like counting letters in a word — they don't natively 'see' individual characters.
interview_question: Why might an LLM struggle to correctly count the number of letters in a word, given how tokenization works?
references: Sennrich et al., Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units, 2016
memory_tip: Subword tokenization = a middle ground: common whole words stay intact, rare words get broken into reusable pieces.
common_mistake: Assuming an LLM 'sees' individual characters the way humans read letter-by-letter — it usually sees subword tokens instead.

question: At a high level, how do diffusion models (used in tools like image generators) work?
answer: Diffusion models are trained to reverse a gradual noising process: during training, images are progressively corrupted with random noise, and the model learns to predict and remove that noise step by step. At generation time, starting from pure random noise, the model iteratively denoises it into a coherent image (or other data type) guided by the learned reverse process, often conditioned on a text prompt.
explanation: This differs fundamentally from GANs (which learn to generate images directly in one shot via an adversarial game) and from autoregressive LLM-style generation (token by token). Diffusion's iterative, gradual refinement process tends to produce very high-quality, diverse samples, at the cost of being slower to generate (many denoising steps) than a single forward pass.
example: Text-to-image tools like Stable Diffusion or DALL-E start from random static and gradually 'sculpt' it into a coherent image over dozens of denoising steps, conditioned on your text prompt via cross-attention.
code:
# Conceptual training step
noisy_image = add_noise(image, timestep)
predicted_noise = model(noisy_image, timestep, text_embedding)
loss = mse_loss(predicted_noise, actual_noise)
real_world: Modern text-to-image and text-to-video generation tools (Stable Diffusion, Midjourney's underlying tech, Sora-style video models) are built on diffusion model principles.
interview_question: Why does diffusion-based image generation typically take more compute at inference time than a single forward pass through a classifier?
references: Ho et al., Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models, 2020
memory_tip: Diffusion = 'sculpt an image out of static, one careful denoising step at a time', guided by what it learned about undoing noise.
common_mistake: Assuming diffusion models generate an image in a single pass like a GAN — the iterative denoising process is central to how they work and why they're relatively slow.
